name: tomato

metadata.name: tomato
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: GNU General Public License v3 or later (GPLv3+)

documentation:
# tomato: au-tomation without pain!

[![Documentation](https://badgen.net/badge/docs/dgbowl.github.io/grey?icon=firefox)](https://dgbowl.github.io/tomato)
[![PyPi version](https://badgen.net/pypi/v/dgpost/?icon=pypi)](https://pypi.org/project/tomato)
![Github link](https://badgen.net/github/tag/dgbowl/tomato/?icon=github)
![Github status](https://badgen.net/github/checks/dgbowl/tomato/?icon=github)

`tomato` is the instrument automation package developed at the [ConCat lab at TU Berlin](https://tu.berlin/en/concat). Currently supported hardware is:

- [`tomato-example-counter`](https://github.com/dgbowl/tomato-example-counter): an example device for testing purposes.
- [`tomato-psutil`](https://github.com/dgbowl/tomato-psutil): an interface to the `psutil` library for CPU and memory usage statistics
- [`tomato-bronkhorst`](https://github.com/dgbowl/tomato-bronkhorst): an interface to the `bronkhorst-propar` library for control of Bronkhorst MFCs and PCs
- [`tomato-jumo`](https://github.com/dgbowl/tomato-jumo): a driver for control of Jumo Quantrol heater controllers using `minimalmodbus`

See the [Documentation](https://dgbowl.github.io/tomato) for more detailed info.

## Contributors:
- [Peter Kraus](http://github.com/PeterKraus)
- [Alexandre Gbocho](https://github.com/AlexN7-Shepard)
- [Loris Ercole](http://github.com/lorisercole)
- [Graham Kimbell](http://github.com/g-kimbell)

## Acknowledgements

This project has received funding from:
- the European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme under grant agreement No 957189.
- the Emmy Noether Programme of the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG) under project No 490703766.
- the Priority Programme (SPP) 2419 HyCAM of the Deutsche Forschungsemeinschaft (DFG) under project No 523873650.

The project is part of BATTERY 2030+, the large-scale European research initiative for inventing the sustainable batteries of the future.

modules.name: tomato.daemon.io
documentation:
# **tomato.daemon.io**: functions for storing and loading data

```
.. codeauthor::
    Peter Kraus
```

functions.name: tomato.daemon.io.store
documentation: Stores the status of the provided {class}`Daemon` as a `pickle` file.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.io.load
documentation: Restores a saved status from a `pickle` file into the provided {class}`Daemon`.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.io.merge_netcdfs
documentation:
Merges the individual pickled {class}`xr.Datasets` of each Component found in {obj}`job.jobpath`
into a single {class}`xr.DataTree`, which is then stored in the NetCDF file,
using the Component `role` as the group label.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.io.data_to_pickle
documentation:
Dumps the data provided as {class}`xr.Dataset` into a `pickle`. Concatenates with
any existing data stored in the `pickle`.

modules.name: tomato.daemon
documentation:
# **tomato.daemon**: module of functions comprising the tomato daemon

```
.. codeauthor::
    Peter Kraus
```

functions.name: tomato.daemon.setup_logging
documentation:
Helper function to set up logging (folder, filename, verbosity, format) based on
the passed daemon state.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.tomato_daemon
documentation:
The function called when `tomato-daemon` is executed.

Manages the state of the tomato daemon, including recovery of state via
{mod}`~tomato.daemon.io`, processing state updates via {mod}`~tomato.daemon.cmd`,
and the manager threads for both jobs ({mod}`~tomato.daemon.job`) and drivers
({mod}`~tomato.daemon.driver`).

modules.name: tomato.daemon.driver
documentation:
# **tomato.daemon.driver**: the driver manager of tomato daemon

```
.. codeauthor::
    Peter Kraus
```

functions.name: tomato.daemon.driver.tomato_driver
documentation:
The function called when `tomato-driver` is executed.

This function is responsible for managing all activities involving devices of a
single driver type.

First, the list of devices (and their channel/address) for the specified driver is
fetched from the `tomato-daemon`. Then, a new instance of the specified driver is
spawned, populating its device map using the above list. If successful, the current
process information is fed back to the `tomato-daemon`.

Afterwards, the main loop handles all requests related to each of the devices
managed by this driver process, including job commands. Finally, if the driver is
instructed to stop, it attempts to perform a teardown before exiting.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.driver.manager
documentation:
The driver manager thread of `tomato-daemon`.

This manager ensures individual driver processes are (re-)spawned and instructed to
quit as necessary.

modules.name: tomato.ketchup
documentation:
# **tomato.ketchup**: command line interface to the tomato job queue

```
.. codeauthor::
    Peter Kraus
```

Module of functions to manage the job queue of {mod}`tomato`. Includes job management
functions:

- {func}`submit` to submit a *job* to *queue*
- {func}`status` to query the status of tomato's *queue*, or a *job*
- {func}`cancel` to cancel a queued or kill a running *job*
- {func}`snapshot` to create an up-to-date FAIR data archive of a running *job*
- {func}`search` to find a `jobid` of a *job* from `jobname`

functions.name: tomato.ketchup.submit
documentation:
Job submission function.

Attempts to open the `yaml/json` file specified in the `payload` argument,
and submit it to tomato's queue.

Reply contains information about the submitted job.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> # Submit a job:
>>> ketchup submit counter_15_0.1.yml
Success: job submitted successfully with jobid 1
```

```pycon
>>> # Submit a job with a job name:
>>> ketchup submit counter_15_0.1.yml -j jobname_is_this
Success: job submitted successfully with jobid 1 and jobname 'jobname_is_this'
```

```pycon
>>> # Submit a job with yaml output:
>>> ketchup submit counter_15_0.1.yml -y
data:
    completed_at: null
    executed_at: null
    id: 1
    [...]
    status: q
    submitted_at: '2024-11-17 19:39:16.972593+00:00'
msg: job submitted successfully with jobid 1
success: true
```

functions.name: tomato.ketchup.status
documentation:
Job status query function.

Reply contains information about all matched jobs.

:::{note}
Calling `ketchup status` with a single `jobid` will return a `yaml`
{class}`list`, even though status of only one element was queried.
:::

# Examples

```pycon
>>> # Get status of a given job
>>> ketchup status 1
Success: found 1 job with status 'qw': [1]
```

```pycon
>>> # Get status of multiple jobs
>>> ketchup status 1 2
Success: found 2 job with status 'qw': [1]
         found 1 job with status 'c' : [2]
```

```pycon
>>> # Get status of non-existent job
>>> ketchup status 3
Failure: found no jobs with jobids [3]
```

```pycon
>>> # Get a status of a job with yaml output
>>> ketchup status 1 -y
data:
  - completed_at: null
    executed_at: null
    id: 1
    [...]
    status: qw
    submitted_at: '2024-11-17 17:53:46.133355+00:00'
msg: found 1 job with status ['qw']
success: true
```

functions.name: tomato.ketchup.cancel
documentation:
Job cancellation function.

:::{note}
The {func}`~ketchup.functions.cancel` only sets the status of the running
job to `rd`; the actual job cancellation is performed in the
{mod}`tomato.daemon.job` module.
:::

# Examples

```pycon
>>> # Cancel a job:
>>> ketchup cancel 1
Success: job [1] cancelled successfully
```

```pycon
>>> # Cancel a job with yaml output:
>>> ketchup cancel 2 -y
data:
  - completed_at: null
    executed_at: null
    id: 2
    [...]
    status: cd
    submitted_at: '2024-03-03 15:23:50.702504+00:00'
msg: job [2] cancelled successfully
success: true
```

functions.name: tomato.ketchup.snapshot
documentation:
Create a snapshot of job data.

Requests an up-to-date snapshot of the data of the job identified by `jobid`.
Checks whether the job is running, raises a warning if job has been finished.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> # Create a snapshot in current working directory:
>>> ketchup snapshot 3
Success: snapshot for job [3] created successfully
```

functions.name: tomato.ketchup.search
documentation:
Search the queue for a job that matches a given jobname.

Searches the queue for a job that matches the `jobname`, returns the
job status and `jobid`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> # Search for a valid jobname
>>> ketchup search counter
Success: job matching 'counter' found: [1]
```

```pycon
>>> # Search for an invalid jobname
>>> ketchup search nothing
Failure: no job matching 'nothing' found
```

modules.name: tomato.models
documentation:
# **tomato.models**: Pydantic models for internal tomato use

```
.. codeauthor::
    Peter Kraus
```

modules.name: tomato.daemon.cmd
documentation:
# **tomato.daemon.cmd**: command parsing for tomato daemon

```
.. codeauthor::
    Peter Kraus
```

All functions in this module expect a {class}`dict` containing the command specification
and a {class}`~tomato.models.Daemon` object as arguments. The {class}`Daemon` object is
altered by the command.

All functions in this module return a {class}`~tomato.models.Reply`.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.cmd.merge_pipelines
documentation:
Helper function for merging a {class}`dict` of new {class}`Pipelines` into the
current {class}`dict`.

modules.name: tomato.daemon.job
documentation:
# **tomato.daemon.job**: the job manager of tomato daemon

```
.. codeauthor::
    Peter Kraus
```

:::{note}
Functions in this module that receive the {class}`~tomato.models.Daemon` state
object should be acting on a copy. All changes to the {class}`Daemon` state have to
be propagated via the {class}`tomato.daemon.cmd` set of functions.
:::

functions.name: tomato.daemon.job.kill_tomato_job
documentation:
Wrapper around {func}`psutil.terminate`.

Here we kill the (grand)children of the process with the name of `tomato-job`,
i.e. the individual task functions. This allows the `tomato-job` process to exit
gracefully once the task functions join.

Note that on Windows, the `tomato-job.exe` process has two children: a `python.exe`
which is the actual process running the job, and `conhost.exe`, which we want to
avoid killing.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.job.manage_running_pips
documentation:
Function that manages jobs and `tomato-daemon` pipelines.

The function only affects pipelines marked as running, i.e. with a set `jobid`.
Jobs scheduled for killing (i.e. `status == "rd"`) are terminated. Jobs that
are supposed to be running but have crashed are given appropriate status (`"ce"`).
Pipelines of both are reset.

Successful job completions are not processed here, but within the job process.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.job.check_queued_jobs
documentation:
Function to check whether the queued jobs can be submitted onto any pipeline.

Returns a {class}`dict` containing the jobids as keys and lists of matched
{class}`Pipelines` as values.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.job.action_queued_jobs
documentation:
Function that assigns jobs if a matched pipeline contains the requested sample.

The `tomato-job` process is launched from this function.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.job.manager
documentation:
The job manager thread of `tomato-daemon`.

This manager ensures the job queue is iterated over and pipelines are managed/reset.
Note that we poll the `tomato-daemon` for status only once per iteration of the main
loop.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.job.tomato_job
documentation:
The function called when `tomato-job` is executed.

This function is resposible for managing all activities of a single job, including
contacting the daemon about job pid, spawning of sub-processes to run tasks on each
Component of the Pipeline, merging data at the end of the job, and reporting back
to the daemon once the job is successfully finished.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.job.job_thread
documentation:
A subthread of `tomato-job`, responsible for tasks on one Component of a Pipeline.

For each task in tasks, starts the task, then monitors the Component status and polls
for data, and moves on to the next task as instructed in the payload.

Stores the data for that Component as a `pickle` of a {class}`xr.Dataset`.

functions.name: tomato.daemon.job.job_main_loop
documentation: The main loop function of `tomato-job`, split for better readability.

modules.name: tomato.tomato
documentation:
# **tomato.tomato**: command line interface to the tomato daemon

```
.. codeauthor::
    Peter Kraus
```

Module of functions to interact with tomato. Includes basic tomato daemon functions:

- {func}`status` to query the status of the tomato daemon
- {func}`start` to start a new tomato daemon
- {func}`stop` to stop a running tomato daemon
- {func}`init` to create a default `settings.toml` file
- {func}`reload` to process the `settings.toml` and `devices.yml` files again

Also includes the following *pipeline* management functions:

- {func}`pipeline_load` to load a sample into a pipeline
- {func}`pipeline_eject` to eject any sample from a pipeline
- {func}`pipeline_ready` to mark a pipeline as ready

functions.name: tomato.tomato.status
documentation:
Get status of the tomato daemon.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> # Status with a running daemon
>>> tomato status
Success: tomato running on port 1234
```

```pycon
>>> # Status without a running daemon
>>> tomato status
Failure: tomato not running on port 1234
```

```pycon
>>> # Status of a running daemon with data
>>> tomato status -y
data:
  appdir: /home/kraus/.config/tomato/1.0rc2.dev2
  cmps:
    [...]
  devs:
    [...]
  drvs:
    [...]
  jobs:
    [...]
  logdir: /home/kraus/.cache/tomato/1.0rc2.dev2/log
  nextjob: 1
  pips:
    [...]
  port: 1234
  settings:
    [...]
  status: running
  verbosity: 20
msg: tomato running on port 1234
success: true
```

functions.name: tomato.tomato.start
documentation:
Start the tomato daemon.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> # Start tomato
>>> tomato start
Success: tomato on port 1234 reloaded with settings from /home/kraus/.config/tomato/1.0rc2.dev2
```

```pycon
>>> # Start tomato with a custom port
>>> tomato start -p 1235
Success: tomato on port 1235 reloaded with settings from /home/kraus/.config/tomato/1.0rc2.dev2
```

```pycon
>>> # Start tomato with another tomato running
>>> tomato start
Failure: required port 1234 is already in use, choose a different one
```

functions.name: tomato.tomato.stop
documentation:
Stop a running tomato daemon.

Will not stop the daemon if any jobs are running. Will create a state snapshot.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> # Stop tomato daemon without running jobs
>>> tomato stop
Success: tomato on port 1234 closed successfully
```

```pycon
>>> # Attempt to stop tomato daemon with running jobs
>>> tomato stop
Failure: jobs are running
```

```pycon
>>> # Attempt to stop tomato daemon which is not running
>>> tomato stop -p 1235
Failure: tomato not running on port 1235
```

functions.name: tomato.tomato.init
documentation:
Create a default settings.toml file.

Will overwrite any existing settings.toml file.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> tomato init
Success: wrote default settings into /home/kraus/.config/tomato/1.0rc2.dev2/settings.toml
```

functions.name: tomato.tomato.reload
documentation:
Reload settings.toml and devices.yaml files and reconfigure tomato daemon.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> # Reload with compatible changes
>>> tomato reload
Success: tomato on port 1234 reloaded with settings from /home/kraus/.config/tomato/1.0rc2.dev2
```

functions.name: tomato.tomato.pipeline_load
documentation:
Load a sample into a pipeline. Usage:

```bash
tomato pipeline load <pipeline> <sampleid>
```

functions.name: tomato.tomato.pipeline_eject
documentation:
Eject any sample present in a pipeline. Usage:

```bash
tomato pipeline eject <pipeline>
```

functions.name: tomato.tomato.pipeline_ready
documentation:
Mark pipeline as ready. Usage:

```bash
pipeline ready <pipeline>
```

classes.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.Attr
documentation: A Pydantic {class}`BaseModel` used to describe device attributes.

classes.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.CreateDeviceManager
documentation:
A factory function which is used to pass this instance of the {class}`ModelInterface`
to the new {class}`DeviceManager` instance.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.dev_register
documentation:
Register a new device component in this driver.

Creates a {class}`DeviceManager` representing a device component, storing it in
the {obj}`self.devmap` using the provided `address` and `channel`.

The returned {class}`Reply` should contain the capabilities of the registered
component in the `data` slot.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.dev_teardown
documentation:
Emergency stop function.

Should set the device component into a documented, safe state. The function is
to be only called in case of critical errors, or when the component is being
removed, not as part of normal operation (i.e. it is not intended as a clean-up
after task completion).

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.dev_reset
documentation:
Component reset function.

Should set the device component into a documented, safe state. This function
is executed at the end of every job.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.dev_set_attr
documentation:
Set value of the {class}`Attr` of the specified device component.

Pass-through to the {func}`DeviceManager.set_attr` function. No type or
read-write validation performed here!

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.dev_get_attr
documentation:
Get value of the {class}`Attr` from the specified device component.

Pass-through to the {func}`DeviceManager.get_attr` function. Units are not
returned; those can be queried for all {class}`Attrs` using {func}`self.attrs`.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.dev_status
documentation:
Get the status report from the specified device component.

Iterates over all {class}`Attrs` on the component that have `status=True` and
returns their values in the {obj}`Reply.data` as a {class}`dict`.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.task_start
documentation:
Submit a {class}`Task` onto the specified device component.

Pushes the supplied {class}`Task` into the {class}`Queue` of the component,
then starts the worker thread. Checks that the {class}`Task` is among the
capabilities of this component.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.task_status
documentation:
Returns the task readiness status of the specified device component.

The `running` entry in the data slot of the {class}`Reply` indicates whether
a {class}`Task` is running. The `can_submit` entry indicates whether another
{class}`Task` can be queued onto the device component already.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.task_stop
documentation:
Stops a running task and returns any collected data.

Pass-through to {func}`DriverManager.stop_task` and {func}`task_data`.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.task_data
documentation:
Return cached task data on the device component and clean the cache.

Pass-through for {func}`DeviceManager.get_data`, with the caveat that the
{class}`dict[list]` which is returned from the component is here converted to a
{class}`Dataset` and annotated using units from {func}`attrs`.

This function gets called by the job thread every `device.pollrate`, it therefore
incurs some IPC cost.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.status
documentation:
Returns the driver status. Currently that is the names of the components in
the `devmap`.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.reset
documentation:
Resets the driver.

Called when the driver process is quitting. Instructs all remaining tasks to
stop. Warns when devices linger. Passes through to {func}`dev_reset`. This is
not a pass-through to {func}`dev_teardown`.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.capabilities
documentation:
Returns the capabilities of the device component.

Pass-through to {func}`DriverManager.capabilities`.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.attrs
documentation:
Query available {class}`Attrs` on the specified device component.

Pass-through to the {func}`DeviceManager.attrs` function.

class_variables.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.devmap
documentation: Map of registered devices, the tuple keys are `component = (address, channel)`

class_variables.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.settings
documentation: A settings map to contain driver-specific settings such as `dllpath` for BioLogic

inner_classes.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.run
documentation: Helper function for starting the {obj}`self.thread`.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.task_runner
documentation:
Target function for the {obj}`self.thread`.

This function waits for a {class}`Task` passed using {obj}`self.task_list`,
then handles setting all {class}`Attrs` using the {func}`prepare_task`
function, and finally handles the main loop of the task, periodically running
the {func}`do_task` function (using `task.sampling_interval`) until the
maximum task duration (i.e. `task.max_duration`) is exceeded.

The {obj}`self.thread` is re-primed for future {class}`Tasks` at the end
of this function.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.prepare_task
documentation:
Given a {class}`Task`, prepare this component for execution by settin all
{class}`Attrs` as specified in the `task.technique_params` dictionary.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.do_task
documentation:
Periodically called task execution function.

This function is responsible for updating {obj}`self.data` with new data, i.e.
performing the measurement. It should also update the values of all
{class}`Attrs`, so that the component status is consistent with the cached data.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.stop_task
documentation: Stops the currently running task.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.set_attr
documentation: Sets the specified {class}`Attr` to `val`.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.get_attr
documentation: Reads the value of the specified {class}`Attr`.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.get_data
documentation: Returns the cached {obj}`self.data` before clearing the cache.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.attrs
documentation: Returns a {class}`dict` of all available {class}`Attrs`.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.capabilities
documentation: Returns a {class}`set` of all supported techniques.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.status
documentation: Compiles a status report from {class}`Attrs` marked as `status=True`.

methods.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.reset
documentation: Resets the component to an initial status.

class_variables.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.driver
documentation: The parent {class}`DriverInterface` instance.

class_variables.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.data
documentation: Container for cached data on this component.

class_variables.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.datalock
documentation: Lock object for thread-safe data manipulation.

class_variables.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.key
documentation: The key in {obj}`self.driver.devmap` referring to this object.

class_variables.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.thread
documentation: The worker {class}`Thread`.

class_variables.name: tomato.driverinterface_1_0.ModelInterface.DeviceManager.task_list
documentation: A {class}`Queue` used to pass {class}`Tasks` to the worker {class}`Thread`.

An abstract base class specifying a manager for an individual component.

This class should handle determining attributes and capabilities of the component,
the reading/writing of those attributes, processing of tasks, and caching and
returning of task data.

An abstract base class specifying the driver interface.

Individual driver modules should expose a {class}`DriverInterface` which inherits
from this abstract class. Only the methods of this class should be used to interact
with drivers and their devices.
